turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion in case 21-476, 303 Creative versus Elenis.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: This case was brought by Laurie Smith, resident of Colorado. Ms. Smith designs custom websites through her business, 303 Creative. The parties to this case agree or as judges like to say, they stipulate that Ms. Smith will serve all customers who come to her business regardless of their race, religion, sex, sexual orientation, or any other status. But the parties also agree that Ms. Smith is careful about which messages she will convey in her expressive work. So, for example, she will not for any client create a website that encourages violence or demeans others, nor will she create a website conveying messages that violate any of our other beliefs. Recently, Ms. Smith sought to expand her business offerings to include customized wedding websites for newly engaged couples. That plan led to this dispute. It is Ms. Smith's belief that marriage should be reserved to unions between one man and one woman and if she enters the wedding website business Ms. Smith worries that Colorado will force her to make websites celebrating same sex marriages. If she refuses, she worries that she will face fines, training mandates, and more. Ms. Smith acknowledges that many disagree with her views about marriage, but she says, the First Amendment protects her right to speak as she thinks. Seeking to clarify her rights Ms. Smith brought this suit. Before us the parties have stipulated to all of the relevant facts at issue. As I noted earlier, they agree that Ms. Smith is willing to work with all persons. They agree that her plan wedding websites involve customized expressive creations, that is, they are protected speech under the First Amendment. The parties have also stipulated that while her work will involve collaboration with clients, the speech Ms. Smith produces will be her own. Additionally, no party before us disputes that if Ms. Smith enters the wedding website business, Colorado will in fact force her to produce speech she does not believe. In the words of the Tenth Circuit's decision below, Ms. Smith faces “a credible threat of prosecution”. In fact, that Court expressly found the Colorado's “very purpose” in threatening such a prosecution is to “eliminate ideas” with which it disagrees. Given these stipulations and findings, the only question we face is this. Can a state for someone who provides expressive services to convey the state's chosen message, even if it defies her own beliefs? The First Amendment exists to protect every person's right to think as she will and speak as she thinks. As this Court has long put it, if there is any fixed star in our constitutional constellation, it is the principle that no government official can prescribe what shall be orthodox in politics, nationalism, religion, or other matters of opinion. To be sure, at times, states have sought to test this foundational principle. Sometimes they've sought to compel speech they thought vital or to suppress speech they considered pernicious. But time after time this Court has rebuffed those efforts. Take a few examples. Eighty years ago, West Virginia forced school children to recite the Pledge of Allegiance and salute the flag. If they refused, the children could be suspended and their parents thrown in jail. In Barnet, this Court held all that violated the First Amendment. Undoubtedly, our decision was unpopular at the time. After all America was in the midst of waging the Second World War and many thought respect for the flag was essential, but speaking for this Court, Justice Robert Jackson did not mince words. The freedom to differ, he wrote, is not limited to things that don't matter much. Fifty years ago, this Court stepped in again Village of Skokie a group of Nazis sought to march through a small town, one home to many Holocaust survivors, all while brandishing swastikas. A state court tried to suppress the march, but this Court rejected that effort, because it said, the state sought to deprive the marchers of rights protected by the First Amendment. Twenty-five Years ago, this Court considered issues that go to the heart of today's dispute. In Hurley, Massachusetts sought to force a veterans group to include a gay pride float in its parade that they were organizing. The veterans objected that the float would convey a message inconsistent with ones they sought to convey. In the end, the Court ruled for the veterans unanimously. Speaking for this Court Justice Souter observed that a parade is not just a march from here to, instead it is intended to express a collective point. No doubt, Massachusetts disapproved the veterans message, but Justice Souter explained that First Amendment forbids of state from forcing speakers to alter their messages. More recent history tells the same story. Five years ago, in Masterpiece Cakeshop, this Court wants more defended controversial First Amendment rights in a case much like ours with Justice Kennedy explaining that “It is not the role of the state or its officials to prescribe what shall be offensive.” Just days ago, this Court held that the First Amendment may even protect stalkers messages to their victims despite the harmful, low value, and upsetting nature of their speech. What do all these cases have in common. They involve unpopular, even hurtful speech, but that did not make a difference. In fact, that was the point. As this Court has emphasized in case after case through decade after decade, all persons are entitled to think and speak as they wish not as the government demands. Those core First Amendment principles resolve today's case just as they have so many in the past. In its decision below, the Tenth Circuit got much of this right. It acknowledged that Ms. Smith's planned weddings websites involve what our cases call pure speech protected by the First Amendment. The Court acknowledged the parties' stipulations that Ms. Smith did not object to working with anyone based on their status. The Tenth Circuit also expressly recognized that if Ms. Smith begins offering wedding websites, Colorado intends to compel speech from her in order to eliminate ideas with which it disagrees. Despite all that, the Court turned away Ms. Smith's First Amendment claim, because it thought Colorado's public accommodations law trumped her free speech rights. In that one particular alone, the Tenth Circuit erred. Colorado's public accommodations law, like statutes in states across the country, seeks to make commercial goods and services available to all comers on non-discriminatory terms. These laws ensure for example that hotels cannot ban guests, trains cannot refuse passengers, and restaurants cannot decline customers based on race, sex, or any other protected trait. Importantly, many states, Colorado included, have expanded their laws in recent years to protect gay persons, just as they protect other classes of individuals. There is much to applaud here. We recognize today as we have many times before the vital work these laws do in helping realize the civil rights of all Americans. At the same time, this Court has also long recognized that a public accommodations law cannot override the First Amendment. Public accommodations laws provide vital protections against discrimination on the basis of status, but that does not mean that a state may use them to police speech in order to eliminate dissenting ideas with which it disagrees. In fact, this Court has made exactly this point before in two prior cases, Hurley and Dale. In both cases, this Court expressly rejected the notion that a state can use its public accommodations laws to force individuals to express ideas they do not hold. The Tenth Circuit erred by failing to acknowledge the force of these precedents. Our colleagues in dissent see things differently. The dissent today even asserts that our opinion authorizes all manner of discrimination and grants businesses the right to refuse customers based on a color of their skin or other legally protected traits. Respectfully, these charges miss their mark. To level them, the dissent must re-imagine the facts of this case and the legal question before us. It must disregard the parties' stipulation that Ms. Smith will work with all persons. It must ignore that everyone agrees Ms. Smith's work as expressive in nature and squarely protected by the First Amendment and nothing like a hotel, restaurant, or other business providing non-expressive goods or services. It must bypass the Tenth Circuit's finding that Colorado seeks to course speech all in order to eliminate ideas with which it disagrees, and it must set aside case after case in which this Court has held that even unpopular speakers may express what they believe not what the government demands. The dissent's embrace of Colorado's effort to eliminate ideas with which it disagrees reflects an unfortunate tendency of some to defend First Amendment values only when they find the speaker's message sympathetic. But just consider where that kind of thinking leads. As Chief Judge Tymkovich asked below, if Colorado could compel the speech at issue here, could a different state compel an unwilling Muslim movie director to make a film with a Zionist message? Could another state force an atheist muralist to accept a commission celebrating evangelical zeal? Could a third state require a gay website designer to create websites for groups advocating against gay marriage? Neither Colorado nor the dissent confronts these uncomfortable questions. Perhaps they trust state governments to compel only enlightened speech. But if that's the calculation, it's a dangerous one indeed. Of course, abiding the First Amendment's commitment to free speech means all of us will encounter ideas we consider deeply misguided. But tolerance not coercion is our nation's answer and in a tolerant society, we do not have to choose between forbidding status based discrimination and protecting free speech. That is why this Court could hold in Bostock that the law protects gay persons from status based discrimination and hold in Hurley that a public accommodations law cannot be used to abridge the veterans right to control their own message. Our nation can and must respect both of these profound commitments. That is the lesson of today's case. In the end, we simply reaffirm what this Court has long held. The First Amendment envisions the United States is a rich and complex place where all persons are free to think and speak as they wish and not as a government demands. For these reasons and others detailed in our opinion filed with the clerk today, the judgment of the Tenth Circuit is reversed. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson had joined.
